## reference · Signal-to-Noise Ratio in UX Design

* The signal-to-noise ratio in UX design refers to the ratio of relevant to irrelevant information in an interface or communication channel.
* **Signal** is relevant and useful information, while **noise** is irrelevant information.
* A high signal-to-noise ratio is crucial for efficient communication and task completion.
* To achieve a high signal-to-noise ratio:
  * Prioritize content using a clear content strategy and content hierarchy.
  * Use a strong visual hierarchy to draw attention to important elements.
  * Remove or minimize elements that serve no purpose or distract users.
* Techniques for creating visual hierarchy include:
  * Using large and bold fonts.
  * Changing colors.
  * Adding icons.
  * Increasing the size of buttons.
* **Avoid**:
  * Redundant content.
  * Large blocks of text.
  * Gratuitous animations.
  * Huge images that don't communicate much information.
* **Consider**:
  * User goals and tasks when determining signal and noise.
  * Business goals and priorities when balancing signal and noise.
  * Consistency in design to help users learn to ignore noise.

Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/signal-noise-ratio/
